```dafny
const PAD: nat := 4

function NumDigits(value: nat): nat { if value < 10 then 1 else 1 + NumDigits(value / 10) }

datatype Id = Id(kind: string, value: nat, width: nat)

predicate Wellformed(x: Id) { x.width >= NumDigits(x.value) }

function Canonicalize(x: Id): Id
{
  Id(x.kind, x.value, if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD)
}

lemma Spec(x: Id)
  requires Wellformed(x)
  ensures Canonicalize(x).kind == x.kind
  ensures Canonicalize(x).value == x.value
  ensures Canonicalize(x).width >= PAD
  ensures Canonicalize(x).width == if x.width >= PAD then x.width else PAD
  ensures x.width >= PAD ==> Canonicalize(x) == x
  ensures Wellformed(Canonicalize(x))
  ensures Canonicalize(Canonicalize(x)) == Canonicalize(x)
{ }
```

**Explanation of key choices:**

- **Implementation:** The canonical width is `max(x.width, PAD)`. Since `PAD == 4`, an id already at width ≥ 4 keeps its width (returned unchanged), and a narrower id is widened to 4. Kind and value are copied verbatim, so the entity never changes.

- **`ensures` clauses:**
  - Kind and value are preserved.
  - Width is always ≥ `PAD`.
  - Width is exactly the max of the original and `PAD`.
  - If already ≥ `PAD`, it's a no-op (`Canonicalize(x) == x`).
  - The result is wellformed: this holds because `Canonicalize(x).width >= max(x.width, PAD) >= x.width >= NumDigits(x.value)`, and value is unchanged. Dafny discharges this from `Wellformed(x)`.
  - Idempotence: canonicalizing twice equals canonicalizing once. After one application width ≥ PAD, so the second application is a no-op — Dafny verifies this by unfolding the `if`.

All clauses follow directly from the structural definition, so verification succeeds without needing helper lemmas.
